1:
On Acts 2:1 — “And when the day of Pentecost was fully come, they were all with one accord in one place” — the witnesses open the account of the Spirit’s descent.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the feast’s significance. Chrysostom sees the type fulfilled: Pentecost was the harvest feast, “the time when the sickle was to be put to the harvest” — and now “came the Spirit down” as the keen sickle of the word. Cyril of Jerusalem marks that the Lord, having ascended, “fulfilled the promise” of another Comforter. Leo calls the day “one of the chief feasts.” The grammatical-historical Robertson glosses “was now come” (en to synplerousthai), “in the being fulfilled completely as to the day”; Ellicott notes it “attracted the largest number of pilgrims from distant lands.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley sets the two Pentecosts side by side: “the Pentecost of Sinai… and the Pentecost of Jerusalem… the legal and the evangelical… the terrible, and the merciful.”

So the witnesses open the birthday of the Church on the feast of harvest. The disciples wait, “with one accord in one place,” for the promised Spirit; and the timing is full of meaning. The witnesses note the fathers’ typology: as Pentecost gathered the firstfruits of the grain, so now the Spirit gathers the firstfruits of the gospel harvest; and as the Law was given at Sinai fifty days after the Passover, so the Spirit is given fifty days after Christ our Passover was sacrificed. The crowd of pilgrims from every nation, drawn to the feast, provides the very audience for the wonder to come. The verse thus sets the scene for the founding gift: the waiting, united disciples; the ordained day; the gathered nations — all converging on the moment when the ascended Lord pours out his Spirit, and the age of the gospel begins.

So the witnesses open the birthday of the Church on the feast of harvest. The disciples wait, with one accord, for the promised Spirit; and the timing is full of meaning. The fathers’ typology stands: as Pentecost gathered the firstfruits of grain, so the Spirit gathers the firstfruits of the gospel harvest; and as the Law was given at Sinai fifty days after the Passover, so the Spirit comes fifty days after Christ our Passover. The crowd of pilgrims from every nation provides the very audience for the wonder to come.
2:
On Acts 2:2 — “And suddenly there came a sound from heaven as of a rushing mighty wind, and it filled all the house where they were sitting” — the witnesses mark the heavenly sign.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imagery. Ellicott sets the sound beside Sinai — “the great and strong wind that rent the mountains on Horeb” (1 Kings 19:11) — and the Spirit who “moved upon” the deep at creation. Robertson: “suddenly” (aphno, only in Acts), and it was “not wind, but a roar… as of the rushing of a mighty wind.” Cyril of Jerusalem hears “a heavenly trumpet… signifying the presence of Him who was to grant power.” The Reformed Calvin: “it was requisite that the gift should be visible, that the bodily sense might the more stir up the disciples.”

The verse gives the first token of the Spirit’s coming. A sudden sound, like a violent rushing wind, fills the whole house. The descent is announced not silently but with a sign that strikes the senses, declaring that something from heaven is at hand.

So the witnesses read in the rushing sound a sensible sign of the Spirit’s arrival. It was not the wind itself but a roar like it — a heavenly portent, recalling, the witnesses note, both the wind of creation and the storm of Sinai, fitting the Spirit who is the breath of God. They mark Calvin’s point that the visible and audible sign was a condescension to human dullness, that the disciples might be roused to perceive the gift and not let God’s power “pass away unknown.” The suddenness, too, befits a free and sovereign gift, unmerited and unexpected. The verse thus marks the dramatic onset of Pentecost: the ascended Christ keeps his promise, and the Spirit’s coming is heralded by a sound from heaven that fills the house and prepares the disciples, and the gathering city, for the greater wonder of the tongues of fire and the gift of speech.

So the witnesses read in the rushing sound a sensible sign of the Spirit’s arrival — not the wind itself but a roar like it, recalling both the wind of creation and the storm of Sinai, fitting the Spirit who is the breath of God. They mark Calvin’s point that the audible sign was a condescension to human dullness, that the disciples might be roused and God’s power not pass unknown. The suddenness befits a free and sovereign gift; the heralding sound prepares the disciples, and the city, for the tongues of fire and the gift of speech.
3:
On Acts 2:3 — “And there appeared unto them cloven tongues like as of fire, and it sat upon each of them” — the witnesses mark the tongues of fire.

The grammatical-historical commentators correct and explain the image. Ellicott: better “tongues as of fire… parted among them” — the word “cannot possibly” mean cloven but means distributing, one to each. Robertson: “parting asunder” (diamerizomena), distributing themselves. Clarke: “the tongues were the emblem of the languages they were to speak… the fire… a spiritual gift.” Wesley: “small flames of fire,” intimating “God’s touching their tongues… with Divine fire.”

The verse gives the second and more personal sign. Flames like tongues of fire appear, parting and resting on each disciple. The symbol is fitting: tongues, for the gift of speech in many languages; fire, for the purifying, kindling power of the Spirit.

So the witnesses read the tongues of fire as the visible emblem of the gift about to be given. The shape — tongues — points to the languages they will speak; the substance — fire — to the Spirit’s purifying and empowering presence. The witnesses note the correction of “cloven”: the flames did not split but distributed, one resting on each, so that every disciple received the gift personally. They mark the fitness of fire, which gives light and warmth and consumes dross, as the token of a Spirit who enlightens, inflames, and purifies. Wesley sees in it God touching their tongues, and their hearts, with divine fire, giving words “active and penetrating.” The verse thus shows the Spirit descending not on the company as a mass but on each believer singly, equipping every one for the proclamation that follows — the gospel to be carried, in the tongues of all nations, to a waiting world.

So the witnesses read the tongues of fire as the visible emblem of the gift about to be given: the shape pointing to the languages they will speak, the substance to the Spirit’s purifying and empowering presence. They note the correction of “cloven” — the flames did not split but distributed, one resting on each, so every disciple received the gift personally. Wesley sees God touching their tongues and hearts with divine fire. The Spirit descends not on the company as a mass but on each believer singly, equipping every one for the proclamation that follows.
4:
On Acts 2:4 — “And they were all filled with the Holy Ghost, and began to speak with other tongues, as the Spirit gave them utterance” — the witnesses mark the gift of tongues.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the miracle. Robertson: “other than their native tongues… a real language… not jargon, but intelligible language.” Clarke sees Babel reversed: “at the building of Babel the language of the people was confounded… at this foundation of the Christian Church, the gift of various languages was given… that the scattered nations might be gathered.” Wesley: “the miracle was… in the mouth of the speakers,” not the ears of hearers. Ellicott: the outward portent was “the sign of a greater spiritual wonder.”

The verse gives the substance of the gift. Filled with the Holy Ghost, the disciples begin to speak in languages they had never learned, as the Spirit prompted. The sign of wind and fire issues in the wonder of speech — the gospel made ready for every nation.

So the witnesses see in the gift of tongues the great purpose of Pentecost: the gospel for all peoples. Filled with the Spirit, unlettered Galileans speak the languages of the world, that the pilgrims of every nation might hear the wonderful works of God each in his own tongue. The witnesses mark the reversal of Babel: where pride once scattered mankind by confounding their speech, grace now gathers them by giving the apostles all speech. They note, with Wesley, that the miracle lay in the speakers’ mouths, not merely the hearers’ ears — a real and intelligible gift of languages. And they mark, with Ellicott, that the outward sign pointed to the inward fullness — the Spirit poured out, not merely a power to speak. The verse thus inaugurates the worldwide mission: the Church, born of the Spirit, is from the first equipped to proclaim Christ to the nations in their own tongues.

So the witnesses see in the gift of tongues the great purpose of Pentecost: the gospel for all peoples. Filled with the Spirit, unlettered Galileans speak the languages of the world, that the pilgrims of every nation might hear the wonderful works of God each in his own tongue. The witnesses mark the reversal of Babel: where pride once scattered mankind by confounding their speech, grace now gathers them by giving the apostles all speech. 5:
On Acts 2:5 — “And there were dwelling at Jerusalem Jews, devout men, out of every nation under heaven” — the witnesses mark the gathered audience.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the composition of the crowd. Robertson: “were dwelling” (periphrastic imperfect) — some settled, some come for the feast. Ellicott notes the word implies “a more settled residence” than mere sojourning, yet wide enough to include festival pilgrims. Clarke and Barnes: Jews of the dispersion and proselytes, born in many lands, come up for Pentecost. The Reformed Calvin: God “in all ages… did gather together into that city some seed which remained.”

The verse describes the providential audience for the miracle. Devout Jews from every nation under heaven were in Jerusalem — the dispersion gathered, speaking the languages of the world, present at the very hour the apostles received the gift of tongues.

So the witnesses see the hand of God in the assembled multitude. The feast had drawn pious Jews and proselytes from every quarter of the known world, each fluent in the tongue of his adopted land; and it is precisely to such a polyglot crowd that the Spirit gives the apostles the gift of languages. The witnesses note the fitness of the providence: the gospel’s first proclamation, in many tongues, falls on hearers who can understand and carry it home. They mark Calvin’s observation that God had always preserved a remnant drawn to Jerusalem to worship. The verse thus shows the stage divinely set: a worldwide audience, gathered by the feast, ready to hear and to witness; so that the wonder of Pentecost would not be hidden but published, and the firstfruits of the nations would be present at the birth of the Church to receive its first sermon.

So the witnesses see the hand of God in the assembled multitude. The feast had drawn pious Jews and proselytes from every quarter of the known world, each fluent in the tongue of his adopted land; and it is precisely to such a polyglot crowd that the Spirit gives the apostles the gift of languages. The witnesses note the fitness of the providence: the gospel’s first proclamation, in many tongues, falls on hearers who can understand and carry it home — the firstfruits of the nations present at the birth of the Church to receive its first sermon.
6:
On Acts 2:6 — “Now when this was noised abroad, the multitude came together, and were confounded, because that every man heard them speak in his own language” — the witnesses mark the gathering crowd.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the “voice” and the wonder. Ellicott: the word for “voice” (phone) is “never used for rumour… always of some utterance” — here the sound that drew the crowd. Robertson: the “excited ‘other tongues’” were heard. Barnes: “were confounded” — “violently moved and agitated.” The Reformed Calvin: the report spread, that “a great multitude came together… into one place” — for had it been scattered, “the miracle had not been so famous.”

The verse shows the effect of the sign on the city. The sound brings the multitude running; and they are confounded, for each hears the apostles speaking in his own native language. The wonder is undeniable and public.

So the witnesses see the crowd drawn together to witness the miracle. Whether the rushing sound or the noise of the many tongues, something brings the multitude; and what confounds them is the unmistakable fact that each, from whatever nation, hears his own language spoken by these Galileans. The witnesses note Calvin’s point that the gathering into one place made the miracle the more notable and beyond dispute — a wonder seen and heard by all at once, not in scattered fragments. The confusion is not the babble of Babel but its opposite: bewildered understanding, each man hearing intelligibly what he could never have expected. The verse thus shows the gift of tongues doing its work — arresting a whole city, gathering a vast and varied audience, and setting them wondering at a sign that demands an explanation, which Peter will shortly give.

So the witnesses see the crowd drawn together to witness the miracle. Whether the rushing sound or the noise of the many tongues, something brings the multitude; and what confounds them is the unmistakable fact that each, from whatever nation, hears his own language spoken by these Galileans. They note Calvin’s point that the gathering into one place made the miracle the more notable and beyond dispute. The confusion is not the babble of Babel but its opposite: bewildered understanding, each man hearing intelligibly what he could never have expected — a sign that demands an explanation.
7:
On Acts 2:7 — “And they were all amazed and marvelled, saying one to another, Behold, are not all these which speak Galilaeans?” — the witnesses mark the astonishment at the speakers.

The grammatical-historical commentators note both the amazement and its cause. Robertson: “were amazed” (existanto, “to stand out of themselves”), the wonder growing. Barnes: “Galilaeans” were “proverbially ignorant, rude… their dialect… barbarous” (Mark 14:70). Ellicott connects it with Paul’s teaching that tongues are “a sign to them that believe not” (1 Corinthians 14:22), filling them with wonder.

The verse sharpens the marvel. The crowd is astonished, and the reason is the speakers: these are Galilaeans — men proverbial for rude speech and no learning — yet they speak the languages of the world. The contrast between the speakers and the gift makes the miracle plain.

So the witnesses fasten on the wonder of unlettered men speaking many tongues. The Galilaeans were despised for their crude dialect and lack of culture; that such men should suddenly speak the polished languages of distant nations could be no natural acquirement but only a gift of God. The witnesses note that this very contrast was part of the sign’s force: the more unlikely the speakers, the more evidently divine the power. They mark, with Ellicott, that the gift of tongues served as a sign to unbelievers, arresting them with astonishment, though the work of conviction would come through the preaching that followed. The verse thus shows the miracle authenticated by the manifest incapacity of the men who wrought it: no one could suppose these Galilaeans had learned such speech; the only explanation was the Spirit poured out — and the crowd is driven to ask what it can mean.

So the witnesses fasten on the wonder of unlettered men speaking many tongues. The Galilaeans were despised for their crude dialect; that such men should suddenly speak the polished languages of distant nations could be no natural acquirement but only a gift of God. The witnesses note that this very contrast was part of the sign’s force: the more unlikely the speakers, the more evidently divine the power. 8:
On Acts 2:8 — “And how hear we every man in our own tongue, wherein we were born?” — the witnesses mark the crowd’s wondering question.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Barnes glosses “wherein we were born” as “in our native language.” Adam Clarke notes some supposed the miracle was “wrought… on their hearers… as if it had been spoken in the language in which he was born,” though the better view places it in the speakers. Ellicott reads here “a composite utterance,” the writer gathering the many expressions of the several nationalities.

The verse gives the crowd’s amazed query. How is it that each hears his own native tongue? The wonder is personal and undeniable — every man, from whatever land, recognises the speech of his childhood on the lips of these Galilaeans.

So the witnesses dwell on the intimate force of the miracle: each hearer met by the gospel in the very language of his birth. The witnesses note the question raised by the wording — whether the wonder lay in the apostles’ speaking or the hearers’ understanding — and follow the better reading, that the apostles truly spoke the many languages, which the hearers then naturally understood. Either way, the marvel is that the wonderful works of God reached every man in his own mother tongue, the speech “wherein he was born.” The verse thus underscores the gracious particularity of Pentecost: the gospel comes not in one privileged tongue but in all, meeting each people where they are. It is the first token of a Church for all nations, and a rebuke to every pride that would confine the word of God to a single language or culture — for at Pentecost the Spirit spoke to all in the tongues of their birth.

So the witnesses dwell on the intimate force of the miracle: each hearer met by the gospel in the very language of his birth. The witnesses note the question raised by the wording — whether the wonder lay in the apostles’ speaking or the hearers’ understanding — and follow the better reading, that the apostles truly spoke the many languages. Either way, the marvel is that the wonderful works of God reached every man in his mother tongue. It is the first token of a Church for all nations, and a rebuke to every pride that would confine the word of God to a single language.
9:
On Acts 2:9 — “Parthians, and Medes, and Elamites, and the dwellers in Mesopotamia, and in Judaea, and Cappadocia, in Pontus, and Asia” — the witnesses mark the first of the nations named.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the geography. Barnes: Luke “enumerates the different nations… In this way the number of languages… can be ascertained” — the list beginning “at the east, and proceeding” westward. Clarke locates Parthia, Media, Elam, and the rest about the Caspian and into Asia Minor. Wesley notes Judaea’s dialect “greatly differed from that of Galilee.”

The verse begins the famous catalogue of nations. From Parthia and Media in the east, through Mesopotamia and Judaea, to the provinces of Asia Minor, the list traces the spread of the Jewish dispersion — and the reach of the miracle.

So the witnesses see in the roll of nations the measure of Pentecost’s scope. Luke records the lands one by one, the witnesses note, to show how many languages the apostles spoke and how wide the gift reached. The list sweeps from the far east of the dispersion westward across the empire, naming peoples scattered since the exile and beyond. The witnesses mark that even Judaea is included — whose speech differed from Galilee’s — so that none could say the wonder was merely the apostles’ own region. The verse thus turns the miracle into a map of the world the gospel will reach: the nations present in miniature at Jerusalem, each hearing the works of God in its own tongue, are the firstfruits and the foreshadowing of the worldwide mission. The Spirit’s gift is no parochial sign but a declaration that the gospel is for all the families of the earth.

So the witnesses see in the roll of nations the measure of Pentecost’s scope. Luke records the lands one by one, the witnesses note, to show how many languages the apostles spoke and how wide the gift reached. 10:
On Acts 2:10 — “Phrygia, and Pamphylia, in Egypt, and in the parts of Libya about Cyrene, and strangers of Rome, Jews and proselytes” — the witnesses mark the further nations.

The grammatical-historical commentators continue the geography. Clarke and Barnes locate Phrygia and Pamphylia in Asia Minor, Egypt and Cyrenaic Libya in Africa. Ellicott: “strangers of Rome” are better “the Romans who were sojourning there,” and “Jews and proselytes” marks that the crowd included both born Jews and Gentile converts to Judaism. Wesley: the Roman sojourners were “born at Rome, but now living at Jerusalem.”

The verse extends the list to Africa and to Rome itself, and notes the two classes present — Jews by birth and proselytes. The reach of the dispersion stretches from Asia to Egypt to the capital of the empire; and the converts to Judaism are gathered in too.

So the witnesses see the catalogue widen to the whole Mediterranean world and beyond. The gospel’s first audience includes Africans from Egypt and Cyrene, sojourning Romans from the heart of the empire, and — significantly — not only Jews but proselytes, Gentiles drawn to the God of Israel. The witnesses note this last detail as a quiet foreshadowing: even at Pentecost the circle is not closed to the Jewish-born alone but includes Gentile converts, anticipating the wider ingathering to come. The presence of Romans, too, points toward the gospel’s eventual march to the capital itself. The verse thus completes the picture of a world in miniature assembled at Jerusalem, every region hearing the wonderful works of God in its own speech — the nations that will one day hear the gospel preached, present at its birth and bearing witness to its first and universal sign.

So the witnesses see the catalogue widen to the whole Mediterranean world and beyond. The gospel’s first audience includes Africans from Egypt and Cyrene, sojourning Romans from the heart of the empire, and — significantly — not only Jews but proselytes, Gentiles drawn to the God of Israel. The witnesses note this last detail as a quiet foreshadowing: even at Pentecost the circle is not closed to the Jewish-born alone. The presence of Romans points toward the gospel’s eventual march to the capital itself — a world in miniature assembled at Jerusalem to witness its first and universal sign.
11:
On Acts 2:11 — “Cretes and Arabians, we do hear them speak in our tongues the wonderful works of God” — the witnesses mark the close of the list and the content of the speech.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the additions and the theme. Robertson: Cretes and Arabians “seem to have been added… as an afterthought.” Ellicott: “the wonderful works of God” means “the great things, or the majesty, of God” — “words of praise and not of teaching.” The Reformed Calvin marks the double wonder: that “ignorant and private persons… born in a base corner” should “intreat profoundly of divine matters,” and that they did so in foreign tongues.

The verse names the last of the nations and tells what was spoken. From Crete to Arabia, all hear in their own tongues “the wonderful works of God.” The content of the Spirit-given speech is praise — the magnifying of God’s great deeds.

So the witnesses see the catalogue close and the purpose of the tongues appear. The gift was not given for display but for praise: the apostles spoke “the wonderful works of God” — his majesty, his mighty acts, doubtless the death, resurrection, and exaltation of Christ and the outpouring of the Spirit. The witnesses note Calvin’s twofold marvel: that lowly Galileans should treat of deep divine things at all, and that they should do so in the languages of the world. They mark, with Ellicott, that the speech was worship, not yet instruction; the teaching would come through Peter’s sermon. The verse thus reveals the heart of the Pentecostal gift: a Spirit-filled people magnifying God in every tongue, the firstfruits of that worldwide praise in which all nations shall one day glorify him. The wonder of speech serves the worship of God and the proclamation of his works.

So the witnesses see the catalogue close and the purpose of the tongues appear. The gift was not given for display but for praise: the apostles spoke “the wonderful works of God” — his majesty and mighty acts, doubtless the death, resurrection, and exaltation of Christ. The witnesses note Calvin’s twofold marvel, that lowly Galileans should treat of deep divine things at all, and in the languages of the world. 12:
On Acts 2:12 — “And they were all amazed, and were in doubt, saying one to another, What meaneth this?” — the witnesses mark the crowd’s perplexity.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the strong word. Robertson: “were perplexed” (dieporounto), “to be wholly at a loss”; “what meaneth this?” is literally “what does this wish to be?” Ellicott: “were much perplexed,” as in Luke 24:4. The Reformed Calvin marks the wonder of the sign: “if God should openly descend from heaven, his majesty could scarce more manifestly appear than in this miracle.”

The verse records the honest bewilderment of the devout. Amazed and wholly at a loss, they ask what the wonder can mean. The sign is undeniable; its significance, they cannot yet grasp; and so they seek an explanation.

So the witnesses see in the crowd’s perplexity the right response of honest hearts to a divine work they do not understand. Unlike the mockers of the next verse, these are amazed and inquiring — they own the wonder and ask its meaning. The witnesses note Calvin’s strong word: that God’s majesty could scarcely appear more plainly than in this miracle, so that to remain unmoved would be monstrous. The question “What meaneth this?” is the opening through which Peter’s sermon will enter; their perplexity is the soil in which faith may be sown. The verse thus marks the gathered crowd at the threshold of decision: confronted with an evident work of God, some honestly seek its meaning, while others scoff. It is to this very question that Peter will rise to answer, interpreting the sign by the prophecy of Joel and leading his hearers from wonder to the knowledge of the crucified and risen Christ.

13:
On Acts 2:13 — “Others mocking said, These men are full of new wine” — the witnesses mark the scoffers’ charge.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the mockery. Robertson: “mocking” (diachleuazontes, “a joke”), with “new wine” (gleukous), sweet but “very intoxicating.” Clarke: gleucus was a peculiar sweet wine. Wesley traces the world’s descent: “the world begins with mocking, thence proceeds to cavilling… to threats… to slaughter.” Cyril of Jerusalem turns the slander to truth: “in truth the wine was new, even the grace of the New Testament… from a spiritual Vine.”

The verse gives the other reaction to the sign — derision. Some, unable or unwilling to understand, dismiss the wonder with a sneer: the men are drunk on new wine. Mockery becomes the refuge of those who will not believe.

So the witnesses see the crowd divide between honest wonder and willful scorn. Where the devout ask “what meaneth this?”, the mockers wave it away with a jest about drunkenness — an absurd charge, as Peter will show, but the easy resort of unbelief. The witnesses note Wesley’s sober tracing of the world’s opposition, which begins in mockery and ends, if unchecked, in murder. They mark Cyril of Jerusalem’s irony: the scoffers spoke truer than they knew, for the disciples were indeed filled with new wine — the new wine of the Spirit, the grace of the New Covenant from the true Vine. The verse thus shows the perennial two-fold response to the work of God: some seek, some scoff; and it sets up Peter’s answer, which will refute the slander and turn the moment of derision into the occasion of the first great gospel sermon.

So the witnesses see the crowd divide between honest wonder and willful scorn. Where the devout ask “what meaneth this?”, the mockers wave it away with a jest about drunkenness — an absurd charge, but the easy resort of unbelief. The witnesses note Wesley’s sober tracing of the world’s opposition, which begins in mockery and ends in murder; and Cyril of Jerusalem’s irony, that the scoffers spoke truer than they knew, for the disciples were filled with new wine — the new wine of the Spirit, the grace of the New Covenant from the true Vine.
14:
On Acts 2:14 — “But Peter, standing up with the eleven, lifted up his voice, and said unto them, Ye men of Judaea, and all ye that dwell at Jerusalem” — the witnesses mark the rise of the preacher.

The Eastern fathers note the divine ordering. Chrysostom: it was “divinely ordered that some mocked, that he might have a starting-point for his defence.” The grammatical-historical commentators mark the change in Peter. Ellicott: “the marvellous change… timidity has become boldness” — for the man who had denied his Lord, “elaborate discourses.” Robertson: he “took his stand with the eleven… a formal and impressive beginning.” Wesley notes the “utmost sobriety” of his gestures and the three-part structure of the sermon.

The verse brings Peter forward as the spokesman of the apostles. Rising with the eleven, he lifts his voice to answer the mockery and address the assembled city. The first sermon of the Christian Church is about to be preached.

So the witnesses mark the transformation of Peter and the beginning of the apostolic preaching. The man who weeks before had denied Christ before a servant-girl now stands boldly before the multitude of Jerusalem to proclaim him. The witnesses note Chrysostom’s point that even the mockery served God’s purpose, giving Peter his opening; and Ellicott’s, that the timid disciple has become the courageous preacher — the fruit of the Spirit just poured out. They observe the formality and gravity of his rising “with the eleven,” the apostles standing together in united witness. The verse thus inaugurates the public ministry of the Word: filled with the Spirit, Peter takes his stand to interpret the wonder, refute the scoffers, and preach Christ crucified and risen. The sermon that follows — the first in the age of the Church — will turn three thousand hearers from mockery and perplexity to faith.

The man who weeks before had denied Christ before a servant-girl now stands boldly before the multitude to proclaim him.
