15:
On Acts 2:15 — “For these are not drunken, as ye suppose, seeing it is but the third hour of the day” — the witnesses mark Peter’s refutation of the slander.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the common-sense reply. Robertson: “a quick, common sense reply, and complete answer to their suspicion”; the third hour is nine in the morning. Clarke and Barnes: before that hour, the hour of prayer, “the Jews scarcely ever ate or drank,” even the intemperate. Ellicott: “drunkenness belonged to the night” (1 Thessalonians 5:7). Cyril of Jerusalem finds a deeper sign in the hour: “He who… was crucified at the third hour, now at the third hour sent down His grace.”

The verse gives Peter’s prompt and pointed answer to the charge of drunkenness. It is only the third hour — nine in the morning, before any Jew would be drunk, especially on a feast day. The slander refutes itself.

So the witnesses admire the readiness and force of Peter’s reply. The mockers had charged drunkenness; Peter answers with a fact all could verify — the early hour, before food or drink, before the hour of prayer was even past. No one was drunk at nine in the morning; the supposition was absurd. The witnesses note that this brisk, sober rebuttal both clears the disciples and prepares the way for the true explanation: not wine, but the Spirit. They mark Cyril of Jerusalem’s devout observation that the very hour was significant — the Christ crucified at the third hour now sends his grace at the third hour. The verse thus sweeps away the scoffers’ excuse and turns the discourse toward its great theme: the wonder is no drunken raving but the fulfilment of prophecy, the outpouring of the Spirit foretold by Joel, which Peter will now expound.

So the witnesses admire the readiness and force of Peter’s reply. The mockers had charged drunkenness; Peter answers with a fact all could verify — the early hour, before food or drink, before the hour of prayer was even past. No one was drunk at nine in the morning; the supposition was absurd. The witnesses note that this brisk rebuttal both clears the disciples and prepares the true explanation: not wine, but the Spirit. Cyril of Jerusalem adds the devout note that the very hour was significant — the Christ crucified at the third hour now sends his grace at the third hour.
16:
On Acts 2:16 — “But this is that which was spoken by the prophet Joel” — the witnesses mark the turn to prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the interpretation. Robertson: a “positive interpretation of the supernatural phenomena in the light of the Messianic prophecy of Joel 2:28” — Peter’s mind “now opened by the Holy Spirit.” Barnes: “this is the fulfillment of that… the second part of Peter’s argument.” Clarke: “the prophecy… is just now fulfilled; and this is another proof that Jesus… is the Messiah.”

The verse gives Peter’s decisive explanation. Not wine, but Joel’s prophecy fulfilled — “this is that.” The wonder the crowd witnesses is the very outpouring of the Spirit foretold of old, the sign of the Messianic age.

So the witnesses see Peter answer the question “what meaneth this?” by appeal to Scripture. The phenomenon is no novelty to be explained away but a fulfilment to be recognised: what Joel foretold of the last days is happening now before their eyes. The witnesses note that Peter’s own mind has been opened by the Spirit to grasp the prophecy and its fulfilment together; the same Spirit who gave the gift now gives the interpretation. They mark the force of “this is that” as the hinge of the sermon’s first movement: the bridge from the wonder to the word, from sign to Scripture. The verse thus establishes the method of all apostolic preaching — interpreting God’s present acts by his prophetic word — and prepares the citation of Joel that follows, which will frame Pentecost as the dawn of the last days and lead, through “whosoever shall call,” to the offer of salvation in Christ.

So the witnesses see Peter answer the question “what meaneth this?” by appeal to Scripture. The phenomenon is no novelty to be explained away but a fulfilment to be recognised: what Joel foretold of the last days is happening now before their eyes. The witnesses note that Peter’s own mind has been opened by the Spirit to grasp the prophecy and its fulfilment together. The force of “this is that” is the hinge from wonder to word, from sign to Scripture — the method of all apostolic preaching, interpreting God’s present acts by his prophetic word.
17:
On Acts 2:17 — “And it shall come to pass in the last days, saith God, I will pour out of my Spirit upon all flesh… your sons and your daughters shall prophesy” — the witnesses mark the prophecy of the outpoured Spirit.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the changes and the sense. Robertson: Joel’s “afterward” Peter renders “in the last days,” defining “those days” as the day of the Lord. Gill cites Kimchi that “afterwards” means “in the last days… the times of the Messiah.” Wesley: “upon all flesh — on persons of every age, sex, and rank.” The Reformed Calvin: the prophecy “belongeth unto the last age alone.”

The verse gives the heart of Joel’s promise. In the last days God will pour out his Spirit on all flesh, so that sons and daughters, young and old, shall prophesy. The gift once confined to a few prophets is now spread abroad upon the whole people of God.

So the witnesses see in Joel’s prophecy the charter of the Pentecostal age. The “last days” are the days of the Messiah, now begun; and their mark is the Spirit poured out not on a chosen few but on “all flesh” — every age, sex, and rank. The witnesses note the great enlargement: where once the Spirit of prophecy rested on select prophets, now sons and daughters alike are made bearers of the Spirit. This is the democratising of grace, the fulfilment of Moses’ wish that all the Lord’s people were prophets. The verse thus interprets Pentecost as the inauguration of a new era in God’s dealings: the age of the outpoured Spirit, in which the whole Church is anointed and the gospel goes to all peoples. Peter’s citation lifts the day’s wonder into its true frame — the last days have dawned, and the Spirit is being poured on all who will receive him.

So the witnesses see in Joel’s prophecy the charter of the Pentecostal age. The “last days” are the days of the Messiah, now begun; and their mark is the Spirit poured out not on a chosen few but on “all flesh” — every age, sex, and rank. The witnesses note the great enlargement: where once the Spirit of prophecy rested on select prophets, now sons and daughters alike are made bearers of the Spirit. Peter’s citation lifts the day’s wonder into its true frame — the last days have dawned, and the Spirit is poured on all who will receive him.
18:
On Acts 2:18 — “And on my servants and on my handmaidens I will pour out in those days of my Spirit; and they shall prophesy” — the witnesses mark the gift to the lowliest.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the inclusion of slaves. Ellicott: “the culminating point… not on priests only… but on slaves, male and female,” from him who is “no respecter of persons.” Clarke: “the Jews asserted that the spirit of prophecy never rested upon a poor man,” yet here “neither bond nor free… is excluded.” The Reformed Calvin: the promise is “restrained unto the worshippers of God,” for “we are made the servants of God by the Spirit.”

The verse extends the promise to the very lowest. Even on bondservants, men and women, God will pour his Spirit, and they shall prophesy. The gift overleaps every barrier of rank, reaching the slave as freely as the free.

So the witnesses see in this clause the crowning grace of Joel’s prophecy: the Spirit given to slaves. The witnesses note Ellicott’s point that this was the culmination — the gift bestowed not on priests or the learned only but on the bondservant, male and female, by a God who is no respecter of persons. They mark Clarke’s observation that the Jews had thought prophecy beyond the reach of the poor, a barrier now swept away. The verse thus declares the radical levelling of the Pentecostal age: in the giving of the Spirit, the distinctions of bond and free, rich and poor, are abolished, and the lowliest believer may be a vessel of God’s word. Calvin’s caution holds — the promise belongs to God’s worshippers, those made his servants by the Spirit — yet within that company none is excluded by earthly station. The verse magnifies the grace that crowns the slave with the gift of prophecy.

So the witnesses see in this clause the crowning grace of Joel’s prophecy: the Spirit given to slaves. The witnesses note Ellicott’s point that this was the culmination — the gift bestowed not on priests or the learned only but on the bondservant, male and female, by a God who is no respecter of persons. They mark Clarke’s observation that the Jews had thought prophecy beyond the reach of the poor, a barrier now swept away. Within the company of God’s worshippers, none is excluded by earthly station; the lowliest believer may be a vessel of God’s word.
19:
On Acts 2:19 — “And I will shew wonders in heaven above, and signs in the earth beneath; blood, and fire, and vapour of smoke” — the witnesses mark the portents foretold.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the signs. Robertson notes “wonders” (terata) joined with “signs” (semeia). Clarke and Wesley refer the portents to “the destruction of Jerusalem… the fearful signs… that preceded those calamities” (Matthew 24:7). The Reformed Calvin reads “the whole kingdom of Christ” in the “great day,” not one coming alone.

The verse continues Joel’s prophecy with images of terror — wonders in heaven, signs on earth, blood and fire and smoke. Alongside the gracious outpouring of the Spirit stand portents of judgment, the dark accompaniment of the day of the Lord.

So the witnesses note that Joel’s prophecy joins grace and judgment, the Spirit poured out and the heavens troubled. The witnesses divide on the precise reference: many, with Clarke and Wesley, see the portents fulfilled in the calamities preceding Jerusalem’s fall; Calvin reads them more broadly of the whole reign of Christ, from his first coming to his last. They agree that “great revelations of grace are usually attended with great judgments on those who reject it” (Wesley). The verse thus sets the outpouring of the Spirit within the larger frame of the day of the Lord, which is a day both of salvation and of reckoning. Peter cites these terrors, the witnesses observe, chiefly for the sake of the promise to which they lead in verse 21 — that amid all the shaking of heaven and earth, whosoever calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved.

So the witnesses note that Joel’s prophecy joins grace and judgment, the Spirit poured out and the heavens troubled. They divide on the precise reference: many see the portents fulfilled in the calamities preceding Jerusalem’s fall; Calvin reads them more broadly of the whole reign of Christ. They agree that great revelations of grace are usually attended with great judgments on those who reject them. Peter cites these terrors chiefly for the sake of the promise to which they lead — that amid all the shaking of heaven and earth, whosoever calls on the Lord shall be saved.
20:
On Acts 2:20 — “The sun shall be turned into darkness, and the moon into blood, before that great and notable day of the Lord come” — the witnesses mark the darkening of the heavens.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the imagery. Barnes: “the shining of the sun is an emblem of prosperity; the withdrawing… an emblem of calamity” (Matthew 24:29). Ellicott: “the phenomena of an eclipse.” Robertson notes Peter interprets these “portents” as bound up with Pentecost and the day of the Lord. Wesley: “before the day of the Lord — eminently the last day,” yet not excluding lesser days of vengeance.

The verse continues the portents with the darkening of sun and moon before the great day of the Lord. The lights of heaven fail; the imagery of cosmic upheaval heralds the decisive day of divine visitation.

So the witnesses read in the darkened heavens the solemn prelude to the day of the Lord. The sun turned to darkness and the moon to blood are, the witnesses note, prophetic emblems of calamity and judgment, such as Christ himself foretold. They observe that Peter cites these signs as belonging to the same Messianic age that Pentecost inaugurates, the “last days” that run from the Spirit’s outpouring to the final day of the Lord. Wesley marks that “the day of the Lord” is eminently the last day, though it may embrace every season in which God comes in vengeance on his adversaries. The verse thus holds the awe of judgment alongside the joy of the Spirit, reminding the hearers that the age of grace is also the age of accountability. Yet the dark portents serve, in Peter’s argument, to set off the bright promise that immediately follows: salvation for all who call upon the Lord.

So the witnesses read in the darkened heavens the solemn prelude to the day of the Lord. The sun turned to darkness and the moon to blood are prophetic emblems of calamity and judgment, such as Christ himself foretold. They observe that Peter cites these signs as belonging to the same Messianic age that Pentecost inaugurates, the “last days” that run from the Spirit’s outpouring to the final day. The dark portents serve, in Peter’s argument, to set off the bright promise that immediately follows: salvation for all who call upon the Lord.
21:
On Acts 2:21 — “And it shall come to pass, that whosoever shall call on the name of the Lord shall be saved” — the witnesses mark the gracious promise that crowns the prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the offer and its breadth. Ellicott: the phrase to “call upon” God is a favourite of Luke and Paul (Romans 10:12; 1 Corinthians 1:2). Robertson: “whosoever” — an indefinite relative, “in any single case.” Wesley: the expression “implies the whole of religion, and particularly prayer uttered in faith… shall be saved — from sin and hell.” The Reformed Calvin: after the terrors, God “show[s] a means whereby salvation may shine before our eyes… if we shall call upon him.”

The verse gives the bright climax of Joel’s prophecy. Amid the wonders and the coming day of judgment, one door of hope stands open: whosoever calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved. The promise is universal and free.

So the witnesses see in this verse the heart of Peter’s gospel offer. The dark portents are not the last word; over against them stands the gracious “whosoever.” The witnesses note the breadth of the promise — no nation, rank, or class excluded; any who call upon the Lord in faith shall be saved. They mark Calvin’s observation that God, having stirred men by terror, now sets salvation before their eyes as near and attainable, requiring only that they call upon him. Wesley reads in “call upon” the whole of true religion, faith expressing itself in prayer. The verse thus turns Joel’s prophecy toward its evangelical goal and supplies the foundation for the appeal Peter will press: the Lord on whose name they must call is the very Jesus they crucified, now risen and exalted — and to call on him is to be saved.

So the witnesses see in this verse the heart of Peter’s gospel offer. The dark portents are not the last word; over against them stands the gracious “whosoever.” The witnesses note the breadth of the promise — no nation, rank, or class excluded; any who call upon the Lord in faith shall be saved. Calvin observes that God, having stirred men by terror, now sets salvation before their eyes as near and attainable. The verse turns Joel’s prophecy toward its evangelical goal: the Lord on whose name they must call is the very Jesus they crucified, now risen and exalted.
22:
On Acts 2:22 — “Ye men of Israel, hear these words; Jesus of Nazareth, a man approved of God among you by miracles and wonders and signs, which God did by him in the midst of you” — the witnesses mark the turn to Christ.

The Eastern fathers note Peter’s tact. Chrysostom: he uses “men of Israel” not for flattery but, having “borne hard upon them, he relaxes a little” before naming Jesus. The grammatical-historical commentators mark the boldness and the proof. Ellicott: “we hardly estimate… the boldness… barely seven weeks” since Jesus died as a slave, “the speaker himself had denied all knowledge of Him.” Clarke: “approved” (apodedeigmenon), “celebrated, famous” — attested by miracles such as no prophet had wrought.

The verse begins the Christological heart of the sermon. Peter names Jesus of Nazareth, a man whom God himself attested among them by mighty works. He appeals to what his hearers knew — the miracles done openly in their midst — as God’s own testimony to Jesus.

So the witnesses see Peter move from the prophecy of the Spirit to the person of Christ. He grounds his case on undeniable public fact: Jesus of Nazareth was “approved of God” — accredited, attested — by miracles, wonders, and signs that God wrought through him before their eyes. The witnesses note the boldness of naming, so soon after the crucifixion, the very man whom the rulers had condemned and the preacher himself had denied. They mark Chrysostom’s observation of Peter’s tact, easing toward his hearers before pressing the charge. The verse thus lays the first stone of the argument: the works of Jesus were God’s seal upon him, known to all present, so that his hearers cannot plead ignorance. From this accredited Jesus, crucified yet attested by God, Peter will build to the resurrection, the exaltation, and the call to repentance.

So the witnesses see Peter move from the prophecy of the Spirit to the person of Christ. He grounds his case on undeniable public fact: Jesus of Nazareth was “approved of God” — accredited, attested — by miracles, wonders, and signs God wrought through him before their eyes. The witnesses note the boldness of naming, so soon after the crucifixion, the very man whom the rulers had condemned and the preacher himself had denied. The works of Jesus were God’s seal upon him, known to all present, so that his hearers cannot plead ignorance.
23:
On Acts 2:23 — “Him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have taken, and by wicked hands have crucified and slain” — the witnesses mark the union of divine purpose and human guilt.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the two truths held together. Robertson: “delivered up” (ekdoton) by Judas, yet “by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God.” Ellicott marks “foreknowledge” as Peter’s word (also 1 Peter 1:2), a coincidence bearing on the speech’s genuineness. Wesley: God “knew all that those wicked men intended… and had power to prevent it” — yet did not, for his own purpose. The Reformed Calvin: the death is named “that the resurrection might the more assuredly be believed.”

The verse joins God’s sovereign plan to man’s wicked deed. Christ was delivered up by the fixed counsel and foreknowledge of God — yet his hearers, by lawless hands, crucified and slew him. The cross was both God’s ordained purpose and their guilty act.

So the witnesses see Peter hold together two truths that human reason struggles to join: the death of Christ was foreordained by God and freely committed by sinful men. The witnesses note that the divine counsel did not excuse the human crime; the same act was God’s appointed plan and the murderers’ real guilt. Peter lays the charge plainly — “ye… have crucified and slain” — yet frames it within God’s eternal purpose, so that the cross appears not as the defeat of God’s plan but its fulfilment. They mark Calvin’s point that the death is stressed to make the resurrection the more certain and wonderful. The verse thus presses both conviction and revelation: the hearers are made to own their guilt in the death of the Messiah, while learning that this very death was the determinate counsel of God — the foundation, as the next verse shows, of the resurrection and the gospel.

So the witnesses see Peter hold together two truths that human reason struggles to join: the death of Christ was foreordained by God and freely committed by sinful men. The witnesses note that the divine counsel did not excuse the human crime; the same act was God’s appointed plan and the murderers’ real guilt. Peter lays the charge plainly — “ye… have crucified and slain” — yet frames it within God’s eternal purpose, so the cross appears not as the defeat of God’s plan but its fulfilment, the foundation of the resurrection and the gospel.
24:
On Acts 2:24 — “Whom God hath raised up, having loosed the pains of death: because it was not possible that he should be holden of it” — the witnesses mark the resurrection proclaimed.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the central claim. Robertson: “apparently this is the first public proclamation… of the Resurrection… at a time it was still possible to test the statement.” Barnes: this was “the main point… God had also raised him up.” Clarke and Wesley read “the pains of death” as the bonds or birth-pangs from which he was loosed, “because the Scripture must needs be fulfilled.” The Reformed Calvin reads “the sorrows of death” as more than bodily — the horror of God’s curse, which Christ bore and overcame.

The verse turns from the cross to the empty tomb. God raised Jesus, loosing the pangs of death, for death could not hold him. The grave that received him could not keep him; the resurrection is the heart of Peter’s testimony.

So the witnesses see Peter proclaim the resurrection as the climax of his argument and the vindication of the crucified. The witnesses note Robertson’s point that this was the first public, testable proclamation of the rising — made openly, in Jerusalem, weeks after the event, where any fraud could be exposed. They mark the striking image of death “loosed”: death held Christ as in birth-pangs, but could not retain him, for it was impossible that the Author of life and the Holy One of God should be held by corruption. Calvin deepens it — Christ bore not only bodily death but the dread of God’s curse, and rose victorious over both. The verse thus sets the resurrection at the centre of the gospel: God reversed the verdict of men, raising the Jesus they slew, and so declaring him Lord and Christ. Peter will now prove from David’s psalm that this rising was foretold.

So the witnesses see Peter proclaim the resurrection as the climax of his argument and the vindication of the crucified. The witnesses note Robertson’s point that this was the first public, testable proclamation of the rising — made openly, in Jerusalem, where any fraud could be exposed. They mark the striking image of death “loosed”: death held Christ as in birth-pangs but could not retain him. God reversed the verdict of men, raising the Jesus they slew, and so declaring him Lord and Christ — a rising Peter will now prove from David’s psalm.
25:
On Acts 2:25 — “For David speaketh concerning him, I foresaw the Lord always before my face, for he is on my right hand, that I should not be moved” — the witnesses mark the appeal to Psalm 16.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the proof-text. Robertson: Peter interprets Psalm 16:8–11 “as written by David and with reference to the Messiah”; David’s experience is “typical of the Messiah.” Barnes: the passage is quoted from the Septuagint “with only one slight… change.” Gill: “the whole psalm belongs to the Messiah, and everything… agrees with him.”

The verse begins Peter’s scriptural proof of the resurrection. David, in the sixteenth psalm, speaks of unbroken trust in God and confidence of preservation — words that, Peter argues, point beyond David to the Messiah. The Old Testament itself foretold the rising of Christ.

So the witnesses see Peter ground the resurrection in prophecy, as he had grounded Pentecost in Joel. The psalm speaks of one who set the Lord always before him, sure of being upheld and not abandoned to the grave; and Peter reads it of the Messiah, of whom David’s own confidence was a type. The witnesses note the apostolic conviction that the Scriptures, rightly understood, testify throughout to Christ; here the psalm of David becomes a prophecy of the resurrection. They mark that Peter quotes it carefully, that his hearers may weigh the words and see that they reach beyond the psalmist himself. The verse thus opens the second great proof of the sermon: that the resurrection was no afterthought or fable but the fulfilment of David’s inspired song — a truth Peter will drive home by showing that David died and was buried, and so could not have spoken of himself.

So the witnesses see Peter ground the resurrection in prophecy, as he had grounded Pentecost in Joel. The psalm speaks of one who set the Lord always before him, sure of being upheld and not abandoned to the grave; and Peter reads it of the Messiah, of whom David’s confidence was a type. The witnesses note the apostolic conviction that the Scriptures, rightly understood, testify throughout to Christ. Peter quotes carefully, that his hearers may weigh the words and see that they reach beyond the psalmist himself to the risen Christ.
26:
On Acts 2:26 — “Therefore did my heart rejoice, and my tongue was glad; moreover also my flesh shall rest in hope” — the witnesses mark the hope that reaches beyond death.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the words and their force. Barnes: the Messiah would “rejoice… preserved amidst the sorrows… and could look forward to the triumph” (Hebrews 12:2). Ellicott: the Hebrew “my glory” (rendered “tongue” by the Septuagint) meant the mind or faculty of speech. Robertson: “my flesh shall dwell… in hope” — the body resting in confidence of rising.

The verse continues the psalm’s testimony. The speaker’s heart rejoices and his very flesh rests in hope — not the despair of one facing final death, but the confidence of one who looks to be raised. The body itself reposes in expectation of resurrection.

So the witnesses see in the psalm’s joy the hope that death could not quench. The Messiah, Peter argues through David’s words, faced the grave not with dread but with gladness, his flesh resting “in hope” of being raised. The witnesses note that this is no ordinary mortal’s resignation but a confident expectation that the body would not see corruption — a hope, Calvin observes, springing from sure trust in God, which alone gives the soul rest amid the terrors of death. They mark the depth of the language: heart, tongue, and flesh all share the hope. The verse thus carries forward the argument that the psalm speaks of one whose body would rest only briefly in the grave, in sure hope of resurrection — a hope fulfilled not in David, whose flesh did see corruption, but in Christ, who rose the third day. The joy of the psalm is the joy of the resurrection foreseen.

So the witnesses see in the psalm’s joy the hope that death could not quench. The Messiah, Peter argues through David’s words, faced the grave not with dread but with gladness, his flesh resting “in hope” of being raised. The witnesses note that this is no ordinary mortal’s resignation but a confident expectation that the body would not see corruption — a hope, Calvin observes, springing from sure trust in God. The joy of the psalm is the joy of the resurrection foreseen, fulfilled not in David, whose flesh saw corruption, but in Christ.
27:
On Acts 2:27 — “Because thou wilt not leave my soul in hell, neither wilt thou suffer thine Holy One to see corruption” — the witnesses mark the key verse of the proof.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the terms. Robertson and Clarke: “hell” is Hades, “the unseen world… the state of the dead,” not the place of punishment. Ellicott connects it with the “Descent into Hell” of the Creed and 1 Peter 3:19. Wesley: Christ’s soul went not to hell but to paradise (Luke 23:43); the sense is “thou wilt not leave my soul in its separate state, nor suffer my body to be corrupted.”

The verse gives the heart of the prophecy. God would not abandon the Holy One’s soul to the realm of the dead, nor let his body see corruption. The two clauses foretell, precisely, the resurrection — soul restored, body undecayed.

So the witnesses fasten on this verse as the linchpin of Peter’s scriptural argument. The psalm declares that God will not leave the Holy One in the unseen world of the dead nor suffer his flesh to corrupt; and this, the witnesses note, was fulfilled exactly in Christ, whose soul returned from Hades and whose body, raised the third day, saw no decay. They are careful, with Robertson and Wesley, that “hell” here means the abode of the dead, not the place of torment, and that Christ’s soul was in paradise; the prophecy concerns his rescue from death, not punishment. The verse thus supplies the precise prediction that the next verses will apply: words that could not be true of David, whose body corrupted in the tomb, but were true of Christ alone. It is the scriptural hinge on which Peter turns the psalm from David to the risen Messiah.

So the witnesses fasten on this verse as the linchpin of Peter’s scriptural argument. The psalm declares that God will not leave the Holy One in the unseen world of the dead nor suffer his flesh to corrupt; and this was fulfilled exactly in Christ, whose soul returned from Hades and whose body, raised the third day, saw no decay. They are careful that “hell” means the abode of the dead, not torment, and that Christ’s soul was in paradise. It is the scriptural hinge on which Peter turns the psalm from David to the risen Messiah.
28:
On Acts 2:28 — “Thou hast made known to me the ways of life; thou shalt make me full of joy with thy countenance” — the witnesses mark the close of the psalm’s testimony.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it of the resurrection and exaltation. Robertson: “though dead God will show him the ways back to life.” Gill and Clarke: “thou hast made known to me the ways of life” means “thou hast raised me from the dead.” Wesley: “thou wilt fill me with joy by thy countenance — when I ascend to thy right hand.” The Reformed Calvin: he was “restored from death to life by the grace of God.”

The verse completes the quotation from Psalm 16. God makes known the ways of life and fills with joy in his presence — words of resurrection and ascension. The path from death leads back to life and up to the joy of God’s countenance.

So the witnesses see the psalm end on the note of life and joy beyond the grave. “The ways of life”, the witnesses note, are the path of resurrection — God showing the dead the way back to life; and the “joy with thy countenance” is the gladness of the exalted Christ at God’s right hand. They mark that these words, like the rest of the psalm, fit not David, who remained in the grave, but the risen and ascended Lord. The verse thus rounds out Peter’s proof-text: from confidence in the face of death (v.25), through hope of the body’s rest (v.26), to the prophecy of no corruption (v.27), and now to the fullness of life and joy in God’s presence. The whole psalm, read of the Messiah, traces the arc from death to resurrection to exaltation — and prepares Peter’s decisive argument that David, being dead and buried, must have spoken not of himself but of Christ.

So the witnesses see the psalm end on the note of life and joy beyond the grave. “The ways of life” are the path of resurrection — God showing the dead the way back to life; and the “joy with thy countenance” is the gladness of the exalted Christ at God’s right hand. They mark that these words fit not David, who remained in the grave, but the risen and ascended Lord. The whole psalm, read of the Messiah, traces the arc from death to resurrection to exaltation — preparing Peter’s decisive argument that David, being dead and buried, spoke not of himself but of Christ.
29:
On Acts 2:29 — “Men and brethren, let me freely speak unto you of the patriarch David, that he is both dead and buried, and his sepulchre is with us unto this day” — the witnesses mark the decisive argument.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the move. Robertson: “I may say… freely” (meta parresias), “with fulness, with boldness.” Ellicott: none could dream the psalm’s words “had been literally… fulfilled in” David, “and it was therefore natural to look for their fulfilment elsewhere.” Barnes: “as David was dead and buried… he must have had reference to some other.” Gill notes Peter now calls them “brethren,” an affectionate appeal.

The verse turns the proof to its point. David, the honoured patriarch, is dead and buried; his tomb stands among them still. The psalm’s promise of no corruption could not be true of David, whose body decayed — so it must speak of another.

So the witnesses see Peter clinch his argument by an undeniable fact: David’s tomb was there to see. The witnesses note the force of it — the psalmist who wrote “thou wilt not suffer thine Holy One to see corruption” had himself seen corruption, his sepulchre a known landmark in Jerusalem. Therefore the words reached beyond David to a greater Son. They mark Peter’s respectful boldness, addressing the crowd as “brethren” even as he overturns their assumption, and speaking “freely” of the revered king. The verse thus drives the proof home: the prophecy of an incorruptible body, plainly unfulfilled in David, demands a fulfilment in the risen Christ. Peter’s appeal to the visible tomb makes the argument concrete and inescapable, and prepares the explicit conclusion that David, as a prophet, foresaw and foretold the resurrection of the Messiah.

So the witnesses see Peter clinch his argument by an undeniable fact: David’s tomb was there to see. The witnesses note the force of it — the psalmist who wrote “thou wilt not suffer thine Holy One to see corruption” had himself seen corruption, his sepulchre a known landmark in Jerusalem. Therefore the words reached beyond David to a greater Son. They mark Peter’s respectful boldness, addressing the crowd as “brethren” even as he overturns their assumption. The visible tomb makes the argument concrete and inescapable.
30:
On Acts 2:30 — “Therefore being a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him, that of the fruit of his loins… he would raise up Christ to sit on his throne” — the witnesses mark David as prophet.

The grammatical-historical commentators note David’s prophetic office and a textual matter. Barnes: “being a prophet… David was inspired” (2 Samuel 23:2), and the oath is from Psalm 132:11. Ellicott and Clarke note that the clause “according to the flesh, he would raise up Christ” is wanting in the best manuscripts; without it the sense runs, “God had sworn… that one should sit upon his throne.” The Reformed Calvin: David spoke of things “removed far from the knowledge of men… revealed unto the prophets.”

The verse establishes David’s authority to foretell the resurrection. As a prophet, David knew God’s sworn promise that one of his descendants would sit on his throne; and so he could speak, by inspiration, of that promised King.

So the witnesses see Peter establish that David, far from speaking only of himself, spoke as a prophet of the coming Messiah. The witnesses note the ground of it: God had sworn to David that his throne would be established in a descendant for ever — a promise fulfilled not in any mortal heir but in the eternal King, the Christ. David, being a prophet, knew this and looked beyond his own death to the enduring kingdom of his greater Son. The witnesses observe the textual point that the explicit phrase about raising up Christ is doubtful in the manuscripts, but the argument stands either way: David foresaw the Messiah’s reign. The verse thus supplies the inspired authority behind the psalm: it was not David musing on his own fate but David prophesying, under oath of God, the resurrection and enthronement of the Christ — which Peter now states outright.

So the witnesses see Peter establish that David, far from speaking only of himself, spoke as a prophet of the coming Messiah. The witnesses note the ground of it: God had sworn to David that his throne would be established in a descendant for ever — a promise fulfilled not in any mortal heir but in the eternal King. David, being a prophet, knew this and looked beyond his own death to the kingdom of his greater Son. It was not David musing on his own fate but David prophesying, under oath of God, the resurrection and enthronement of the Christ.
31:
On Acts 2:31 — “He seeing this before spake of the resurrection of Christ, that his soul was not left in hell, neither his flesh did see corruption” — the witnesses mark the explicit conclusion.

The grammatical-historical commentators state the point. Robertson: “foreseeing” (proidon), “did it as a prophet… a definite statement by Peter that David knew that in Psalm 16 he was describing the resurrection of the Messiah.” Barnes: “David had distinct views of the great doctrines pertaining to the Messiah” (1 Peter 1:11). Clarke notes a textual variant in “his soul,” doubtful in the best manuscripts.

The verse states plainly what the argument has been building toward. David, foreseeing it by the Spirit, spoke of the resurrection of the Christ — that his soul was not left in the realm of the dead, nor his flesh corrupted. The psalm was a prophecy of the rising of the Messiah.

So the witnesses see Peter draw his conclusion in so many words: David foresaw and foretold the resurrection of Christ. The witnesses note the definiteness of the statement — the psalm was not David’s reflection on his own end but his prophetic vision of the Messiah’s rescue from death and decay. They mark that this fulfils the principle the apostles everywhere assume: that the prophets, by the Spirit of Christ in them, testified beforehand of his sufferings and the glory to follow. The verse thus completes the scriptural proof of the resurrection: the very words of Psalm 16, impossible of David, were spoken by David as a prophet of Christ, and have now been fulfilled. Peter has shown both that the Messiah must rise (from prophecy) and, as the next verse declares, that Jesus has risen (from eyewitness testimony) — the two halves of his unanswerable argument.

So the witnesses see Peter draw his conclusion in so many words: David foresaw and foretold the resurrection of Christ. The witnesses note the definiteness of the statement — the psalm was not David’s reflection on his own end but his prophetic vision of the Messiah’s rescue from death and decay. They mark that this fulfils the principle the apostles everywhere assume: that the prophets, by the Spirit of Christ in them, testified beforehand of his sufferings and the glory to follow. Peter has shown both that the Messiah must rise and that Jesus has risen.
32:
On Acts 2:32 — “This Jesus hath God raised up, whereof we all are witnesses” — the witnesses mark the apostolic testimony.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the eyewitness claim. Robertson: “this Jesus” — the Nazarene of verse 22, foretold in Psalm 16, now raised. Ellicott: “from the first the Apostles take up the position… they are witnesses… before and above all else, witnesses of the Resurrection.” Clarke: “the whole 120 saw him after he rose… ready… to attest this great truth.” The Reformed Calvin: they “saw him with their eyes after his resurrection.”

The verse joins prophecy to fact. The Jesus whom David foretold and whom they crucified, God has raised — and of this the apostles are eyewitnesses. To the proof from Scripture Peter adds the proof from testimony.

So the witnesses see the two pillars of Peter’s argument joined: the resurrection was foretold, and the resurrection is attested. The witnesses note that the apostles’ primary office, from the first, was to be witnesses of the rising — men who had seen the risen Lord and were ready to seal their testimony with their lives. They mark the force of “we all are witnesses”: not one man’s claim but the united testimony of the whole company, made publicly where it could be tested. The verse thus completes the proof: Scripture predicted that the Messiah would rise; the apostles certify that this Jesus has risen. Prophecy and eyewitness meet in the one conclusion that the crucified Nazarene is the living Christ. On this foundation Peter will build the climax of his sermon — that the risen Jesus, exalted to God’s right hand, has poured out the very Spirit they now behold.

So the witnesses see the two pillars of Peter’s argument joined: the resurrection was foretold, and the resurrection is attested. The witnesses note that the apostles’ primary office, from the first, was to be witnesses of the rising — men who had seen the risen Lord and were ready to seal their testimony with their lives. The force of “we all are witnesses” is not one man’s claim but the united testimony of the whole company, made publicly where it could be tested. Prophecy and eyewitness meet in the one conclusion that the crucified Nazarene is the living Christ.
33:
On Acts 2:33 — “Therefore being by the right hand of God exalted, and having received of the Father the promise of the Holy Ghost, he hath shed forth this, which ye now see and hear” — the witnesses mark the exaltation and the gift.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the exaltation. Barnes reads “by the right hand” as “by his power” — raised and borne to heaven; Robertson allows the instrumental or the locative (“to the right hand”). Clarke: “raised by omnipotence to the highest dignity… to administer the laws of both worlds”; the promise of the Spirit (John 14:16). The Reformed Calvin marks “a wonderful work of God, in that he had exalted his Christ… unto so great glory.”

The verse reaches the climax of Peter’s argument. The risen Jesus, exalted to God’s right hand, has received from the Father the promised Spirit and poured him out — and this is what the crowd now sees and hears. Pentecost itself is the proof of Christ’s exaltation.

So the witnesses see Peter bring his sermon to its height: the very wonder that began the day is the work of the exalted Christ. Risen and ascended to the right hand of God — the place of supreme power and honour — Jesus received the promised Spirit and has now shed him forth on his people. The witnesses note the force of the argument: the tongues and the praise the crowd witnesses are not unexplained marvels but the visible evidence that Jesus reigns; he could not pour out the Spirit unless he were exalted, and he is exalted because God raised him. They mark that the gift fulfils Christ’s own promise of the Comforter. The verse thus binds the whole sermon together: the crucified Jesus is risen, the risen Jesus is exalted, and the exalted Jesus has sent the Spirit — so that Pentecost is the seal of his lordship, which the next verses will confirm from David’s psalm of the Lord at God’s right hand.

So the witnesses see Peter bring his sermon to its height: the very wonder that began the day is the work of the exalted Christ. Risen and ascended to the right hand of God, Jesus received the promised Spirit and has now shed him forth. The witnesses note the force of the argument: the tongues and praise the crowd witnesses are the visible evidence that Jesus reigns; he could not pour out the Spirit unless he were exalted. The gift fulfils Christ’s own promise of the Comforter, and Pentecost is the seal of his lordship.
34:
On Acts 2:34 — “For David is not ascended into the heavens: but he saith himself, The Lord said unto my Lord, Sit thou on my right hand” — the witnesses mark the proof from Psalm 110.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the argument. Robertson: “not David ascended… Peter quotes Psalm 110:1 as proof… No passage in the O.T. is so constantly quoted as Messianic.” Ellicott marks the striking reproduction of the very words by which Jesus had confounded the scribes (Matthew 22:44). Cyril of Jerusalem: “the Lord says this to the Lord, not to a servant, but to the Lord of all, and His own Son.”

The verse applies to the exaltation the same method used for the resurrection. As David did not himself rise incorrupt, so David did not himself ascend; yet he wrote of one whom the LORD bade sit at his right hand. The psalm of the enthroned Lord points beyond David to Christ.

So the witnesses see Peter prove the exaltation from Scripture as he proved the resurrection. David did not ascend to heaven; yet in the hundred-and-tenth psalm he calls another “my Lord,” whom the LORD invites to his right hand. The witnesses note that this is the most frequently cited Messianic text, and that Peter uses the very words by which Christ had silenced the scribes about David’s Son and David’s Lord. They mark Cyril of Jerusalem’s point that the words are spoken by the Lord to the Lord — the Father to the Son, not to any servant. The verse thus grounds the exaltation in prophecy: as David foretold the Messiah’s rising, so he foretold his enthronement at God’s right hand. The Spirit poured out at Pentecost is the act of this enthroned Lord — and Peter is now ready to draw the sermon’s overwhelming conclusion.

So the witnesses see Peter prove the exaltation from Scripture as he proved the resurrection. David did not ascend to heaven; yet in the hundred-and-tenth psalm he calls another “my Lord,” whom the LORD invites to his right hand. The witnesses note that this is the most frequently cited Messianic text, and that Peter uses the very words by which Christ had silenced the scribes. They mark Cyril of Jerusalem’s point that the words are spoken by the Lord to the Lord — the Father to the Son. The Spirit poured out is the act of this enthroned Lord.
35:
On Acts 2:35 — “Until I make thy foes thy footstool” — the witnesses mark the promised subjugation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the image and its force. Clarke: “it was usual with conquerors to put their feet on the necks of vanquished leaders… emblematical of… subjection”; Peter shows the unbelieving Jews their “ruin… if they went on to oppose Christ.” Robertson: this dominion lasts “till the plan of the kingdom is carried out” (1 Corinthians 15:25). Wesley: the text “suggesting… how certain their own ruin must be, if they went on to oppose Christ.”

The verse completes the psalm’s citation. The exalted Lord sits at God’s right hand until all his enemies are made his footstool. The enthronement carries with it the promise of total triumph over every foe.

So the witnesses see in the footstool the assured conquest of the reigning Christ. The Lord whom David calls his Lord sits enthroned until every enemy is subdued beneath his feet. The witnesses note the pointed warning Clarke and Wesley draw for the hearers: the very men now opposing Christ are among the foes who will be made his footstool, so that to continue in unbelief is to court certain ruin. They mark that the reign endures until the kingdom’s purpose is complete and all opposition crushed. The verse thus deepens the sermon’s claim of Christ’s lordship: he is not merely raised and exalted but enthroned in triumph, his ultimate victory guaranteed by God’s own oath. It sets the stage for Peter’s climactic conclusion — that this exalted, reigning Lord is the very Jesus they crucified, whom God has made both Lord and Christ.

So the witnesses see in the footstool the assured conquest of the reigning Christ. The Lord whom David calls his Lord sits enthroned until every enemy is subdued beneath his feet. The witnesses note the pointed warning Clarke and Wesley draw: the very men now opposing Christ are among the foes who will be made his footstool, so that to continue in unbelief is to court certain ruin. The reign endures until the kingdom’s purpose is complete — Christ enthroned in triumph, his ultimate victory guaranteed by God’s own oath.
36:
On Acts 2:36 — “Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly, that God hath made that same Jesus, whom ye have crucified, both Lord and Christ” — the witnesses mark the sermon’s climax.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the force of the conclusion. Ellicott marks “the rhetorical force in the very order of the words… both Lord and Christ hath God made… this Jesus whom ye crucified.” Clarke summarises Peter’s proof — the resurrection, ascension, exaltation, and outpouring — converging here. The Reformed Calvin: Jesus, “whom they had so spitefully handled… is he whom they ought to acknowledge to be their Lord.”

The verse delivers the conclusion of the whole argument. Let all Israel know for certain: the very Jesus they crucified, God has made both Lord and Christ. The crucified is the exalted; the rejected is the reigning Messiah and Lord.

So the witnesses see the sermon reach its overwhelming point. All that has gone before — the Spirit poured out, the resurrection proved and attested, the exaltation foretold and fulfilled — converges in this declaration: God has made the crucified Jesus both Lord and Christ. The witnesses note the deliberate sting in the order of words: “both Lord and Christ… this Jesus whom ye crucified.” The hearers had condemned him as an impostor; God has enthroned him as Messiah and Lord. They mark Calvin’s point that the very one they detested they must now acknowledge and revere. The verse thus presses the hearers to a verdict: the Jesus of Nazareth they slew is alive, exalted, and reigning, the Lord on whose name they must call to be saved. It is this thrust that, in the next verse, pierces their hearts and brings the cry, “What shall we do?”

So the witnesses see the sermon reach its overwhelming point. All that has gone before — the Spirit poured out, the resurrection proved, the exaltation foretold and fulfilled — converges in this declaration: God has made the crucified Jesus both Lord and Christ. The witnesses note the deliberate sting in the order of words: “both Lord and Christ… this Jesus whom ye crucified.” They mark Calvin’s point that the very one they detested they must now acknowledge and revere. It is this thrust that, in the next verse, pierces their hearts.
37:
On Acts 2:37 — “Now when they heard this, they were pricked in their heart, and said unto Peter and to the rest of the apostles, Men and brethren, what shall we do?” — the witnesses mark the conviction of the hearers.

The Eastern fathers note the power of the word. Chrysostom: “more than any vehemence,” gentleness “pricks our hearts, inflicts a keener wound.” The grammatical-historical commentators mark the sharp word. Robertson: “pricked” (katenygesan), “to pierce, to sting sharply, to stun.” Ellicott: it “expresses the sharp, painful emotion… compunction.” Barnes: “no fanaticism… cool, close, pungent reasoning.” Wesley notes they now call the apostles “brethren,” as they had not before.

The verse records the effect of the sermon. The hearers are pierced to the heart and cry out, “What shall we do?” The word, driven home by the Spirit, has turned mockery and perplexity into anguished conviction and a longing to be saved.

So the witnesses see the first great harvest of the Spirit in the convicted hearts of the crowd. Peter’s reasoning, sober and scriptural, has under the Spirit’s power stung them to the quick; they feel the guilt of crucifying their Lord and cry out for direction. The witnesses note Chrysostom’s insight that it was the gentle, reasoned word, not vehemence, that wounded most deeply; and Calvin’s, that the Spirit’s power was shown not only in the tongues but in these pierced hearts. They mark the change in the crowd’s address — now “brethren” — and the turn from derision to earnest inquiry. The verse thus shows true conviction of sin: not despair only, but a humbled question, “what shall we do?” It is the fruit the gospel aims at, the broken and seeking heart — and it opens the way for Peter’s gracious answer, the call to repent and be baptized.

So the witnesses see the first great harvest of the Spirit in the convicted hearts of the crowd. Peter’s reasoning, sober and scriptural, has under the Spirit’s power stung them to the quick; they feel the guilt of crucifying their Lord and cry out for direction. The witnesses note Chrysostom’s insight that it was the gentle, reasoned word, not vehemence, that wounded most deeply. True conviction of sin is not despair only, but a humbled question, “what shall we do?” — the broken and seeking heart the gospel aims at.
38:
On Acts 2:38 — “Then Peter said unto them, Repent, and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the remission of sins, and ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost” — the witnesses mark the gospel answer.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the call. Robertson: “repent” (ingressive aorist), “change your mind and your life… turn right about and do it now”; “let each one of you be baptized.” Clarke: “humble yourselves… deeply deplore the sins.” Ellicott connects it with John’s baptism — “the same indispensable condition of repentance.” Wesley: “repent — and hereby return to God… the gift of the Holy Ghost” not here the tongues but the saving Spirit.

The verse gives Peter’s direction to the convicted crowd. Repent and be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ, for the forgiveness of sins; and you shall receive the gift of the Holy Spirit. To the question “what shall we do?” comes the gospel’s gracious answer.

So the witnesses see in Peter’s reply the way of salvation opened to the very men who crucified Christ. The conditions are repentance — a real turning of mind and life from sin to God — and baptism in the name of Jesus, the public confession of faith in him; and the promised blessings are the remission of sins and the gift of the Spirit. The witnesses note the wonder of it: those who had killed the Lord are offered pardon and the indwelling Spirit, the same Spirit whose outpouring they had just witnessed. They mark, with Wesley, that “the gift of the Holy Ghost” here means the saving, sanctifying Spirit, not merely the power of tongues. The verse thus sets out the abiding pattern of gospel response: repent, believe and be baptized, and receive forgiveness and the Spirit. It is the door through which three thousand will pass that very day — the mercy of God reaching even the crucifiers of his Son.

So the witnesses see in Peter’s reply the way of salvation opened to the very men who crucified Christ. The conditions are repentance — a real turning of mind and life — and baptism in the name of Jesus, the public confession of faith; and the promised blessings are remission of sins and the gift of the Spirit. The witnesses note the wonder of it: those who had killed the Lord are offered pardon and the indwelling Spirit. The verse sets out the abiding pattern of gospel response, the door through which three thousand will pass that very day.
39:
On Acts 2:39 — “For the promise is unto you, and to your children, and to all that are afar off, even as many as the Lord our God shall call” — the witnesses mark the breadth of the promise.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the widening circle. Robertson: “to you” (Jews), “to your descendants,” and “to all that are afar off” — “the horizon widens and includes the Gentiles.” Ellicott: it is “the essence of St. Peter’s appeal that all… can claim the promise as fully as himself,” against every sectarian narrowing. The Reformed Calvin: this was added that the Jews “might certainly… persuade themselves that the grace of Christ did belong as well to them.”

The verse grounds the call in the breadth of God’s promise. The gift is for them, for their children, and for all who are far off — as many as God shall call. No barrier of generation or distance limits the grace now offered.

So the witnesses see the gospel promise flung open wide. Peter assures the convicted hearers that the blessing he offers is not for a privileged few but for them, their offspring, and the distant nations — all whom the Lord effectually calls. The witnesses note the expanding horizon: “afar off” reaches beyond the Jews to the Gentiles, foreshadowing the worldwide scope of the gospel that Peter himself would later carry to Cornelius. They mark Ellicott’s point that the appeal cuts against every attempt to confine grace to an exclusive circle: the promise is as full for the latest and farthest as for the apostles themselves. The verse thus widens the call of verse 38 to its utmost reach, joining the freeness of the offer to the sovereignty of God’s calling. It assures every hearer, near or far, then or now, that the promise of forgiveness and the Spirit is for them, if God call and they answer.

So the witnesses see the gospel promise flung open wide. Peter assures the convicted hearers that the blessing is not for a privileged few but for them, their offspring, and the distant nations — all whom the Lord effectually calls. The witnesses note the expanding horizon: “afar off” reaches beyond the Jews to the Gentiles, foreshadowing the worldwide scope of the gospel. The appeal cuts against every attempt to confine grace to an exclusive circle, joining the freeness of the offer to the sovereignty of God’s calling.
40:
On Acts 2:40 — “And with many other words did he testify and exhort, saying, Save yourselves from this untoward generation” — the witnesses mark the urgent appeal.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the summary and the plea. Robertson: “many other words” — Luke gives only an outline, “more than these.” Ellicott: “the report breaks off… he continued to testify”; “save yourselves” is passive, “be ye saved.” Clarke: “separate yourselves… the power is present with you.” Wesley: “in such an accepted time we should add line upon line.”

The verse marks the earnestness of Peter’s continuing appeal. With many more words he testified and exhorted, urging them to be saved from this crooked generation. Luke gives but the outline; the substance was a pressing call to flee impending ruin and lay hold of salvation.

So the witnesses see Peter press home the gospel with urgency and persistence. The recorded sermon, the witnesses note, is only a summary; Peter went on testifying and exhorting, heaping word upon word to win his hearers. The plea “save yourselves from this untoward generation” is, in the Greek, “be ye saved” — a call to submit to God’s way of salvation and so be delivered, both from the guilt they shared and from the judgment coming on a perverse and unbelieving nation. The witnesses mark the pastoral earnestness Wesley commends, adding line upon line in the accepted time. The verse thus shows that gospel preaching is not bare statement but pressing entreaty: having declared the truth, Peter beseeches them to act on it, to separate from the doomed generation and be saved. It is this urgent appeal that bears fruit in the great ingathering of the next verse.

So the witnesses see Peter press home the gospel with urgency and persistence. The recorded sermon is only a summary; Peter went on testifying and exhorting, heaping word upon word to win his hearers. The plea “save yourselves from this untoward generation” is, in the Greek, “be ye saved” — a call to submit to God’s way and be delivered from the judgment coming on a perverse nation. Gospel preaching is not bare statement but pressing entreaty: having declared the truth, Peter beseeches them to act on it.
41:
On Acts 2:41 — “Then they that gladly received his word were baptized: and the same day there were added unto them about three thousand souls” — the witnesses mark the great ingathering.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the glad reception. Barnes: “gladly” means “freely, cheerfully, joyfully… without compulsion” — “religion is not compulsion.” Robertson: “were baptized” and added. Lightfoot links the “willing people in the day of thy power” (Psalm 110:3). The Reformed Calvin: this “one sermon… gained unto Christ about three thousand men… with a prompt and ready mind they embraced his word.”

The verse records the harvest of Pentecost. Those who gladly received the word were baptized, and about three thousand souls were added that very day. The first sermon of the Church reaps a great and joyful ingathering.

So the witnesses marvel at the fruit of the Spirit’s first day: three thousand converts at one sermon. The witnesses note the word “gladly” — the converts received the gospel not by constraint but with cheerful, willing faith, the mark, Calvin says, of true believing. They mark the immediacy and the scale: the same day, three thousand baptized and joined to the company of disciples. Lightfoot hears the fulfilment of the psalm’s “willing people in the day of thy power” — the day of Christ’s resurrection power now displayed in this multitude won to him. The verse thus shows the gospel’s power to gather a great harvest where the Spirit works: from a city that days before had crucified the Lord, three thousand turn to him in a single day. It is the firstfruits of the worldwide ingathering Pentecost inaugurates, and the proof that the exalted Christ, by his Spirit, builds his Church.

So the witnesses marvel at the fruit of the Spirit’s first day: three thousand converts at one sermon. The witnesses note the word “gladly” — the converts received the gospel not by constraint but with cheerful, willing faith, the mark of true believing. They mark the immediacy and scale: the same day, three thousand baptized and joined to the disciples. From a city that days before had crucified the Lord, three thousand turn to him in a single day — the firstfruits of the worldwide ingathering, and proof that the exalted Christ builds his Church.
42:
On Acts 2:42 — “And they continued stedfastly in the apostles’ doctrine and fellowship, and in breaking of bread, and in prayers” — the witnesses mark the life of the new community.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the four marks. Robertson: “continued steadfastly” (proskarterountes); “fellowship” (koinonia), “partnership… participation in” common life. Clarke distinguishes the “fellowship” from the community of goods. The Puritan Henry: the truly primitive church “kept close to holy ordinances, and abounded in all instances of pious devotion.” Wesley: “their daily Church communion consisted in these four particulars.”

The verse describes the devotion of the first converts. They persevered in the apostles’ teaching, in fellowship, in the breaking of bread, and in prayers. The new community is marked from the first by steadfast continuance in the ordinances of grace.

So the witnesses see in this verse the portrait of the truly primitive church in its purest days. The three thousand did not scatter but continued steadfastly in four things: the apostles’ doctrine, the rule of their faith; fellowship, the shared life and partnership of believers; the breaking of bread, whether the common meal or the Lord’s Supper; and prayers, their united devotion. The witnesses note the emphasis on perseverance — not a passing enthusiasm but a settled continuance — and Henry’s observation that the infant church abounded in pious devotion and kept close to the ordinances. They mark these four as the abiding marks of healthy Christian community in every age. The verse thus shows that the harvest of Pentecost was not merely a moment of conversion but the birth of a devoted fellowship, rooted in apostolic teaching, mutual love, sacramental worship, and prayer — the pattern of the Church.

So the witnesses see in this verse the portrait of the truly primitive church in its purest days. The three thousand did not scatter but continued steadfastly in four things: the apostles’ doctrine, the rule of their faith; fellowship, the shared life of believers; the breaking of bread; and prayers. The witnesses note the emphasis on perseverance — not a passing enthusiasm but a settled continuance — and Henry’s observation that the infant church abounded in pious devotion. These four are the abiding marks of healthy Christian community in every age.
43:
On Acts 2:43 — “And fear came upon every soul: and many wonders and signs were done by the apostles” — the witnesses mark the awe and the miracles.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the reverence and the continuing signs. Barnes: “great reverence or awe… so striking… the power of God… silenced all clamours.” Robertson: “came” and “were done” are imperfect — “awe kept on coming… signs and wonders kept on coming… the more wonders the more fear.” Ellicott notes the careful tenses — fear came “specially at that season,” the signs “wrought continually.”

The verse describes the atmosphere around the young church. A holy fear fell on every soul, and the apostles continued to work many wonders and signs. The same divine power that began at Pentecost goes on, confirming the word and commanding reverence.

So the witnesses see the new community surrounded by awe and attended by miracles. The fear that came on every soul, the witnesses note, was not terror but reverence — the awe inspired by manifest tokens of God’s presence, which silenced the earlier mockery and held opposition in check. They mark Robertson’s observation of the continuous tenses: the wonder grew and the signs multiplied together, the apostolic miracles authenticating the message and deepening the general reverence. Calvin sees in this awe a means of preserving and furthering the church. The verse thus shows the divine power of Pentecost continuing in the life of the church: the apostles’ signs confirm their doctrine, and a holy fear rests on the city, so that the infant community is both attested by miracle and shielded by reverence as it grows.

So the witnesses see the new community surrounded by awe and attended by miracles. The fear that came on every soul was not terror but reverence — the awe inspired by manifest tokens of God’s presence, which silenced the earlier mockery. They mark Robertson’s observation of the continuous tenses: the wonder grew and the signs multiplied together, authenticating the message. The divine power of Pentecost continues in the church’s life: the apostles’ signs confirm their doctrine, and a holy fear rests on the city as the community grows.
44:
On Acts 2:44 — “And all that believed were together, and had all things common” — the witnesses mark the unity and sharing of the believers.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the “common.” Robertson: “not actual communism, but they held all their property ready for use for the common good”; “were together” in the same place and purpose. Barnes: “united in the same community… not that they lived in the same house.” Ellicott sees “a literal fulfilment” of Luke 12:33, a society founded “on sympathy and self-denial.”

The verse describes the remarkable fellowship of the first believers. United in heart and place, they held their goods as common — not by compulsion or formal communism, but by a love that counted nothing too dear to share with a brother in need.

So the witnesses see in the believers’ sharing the fruit of their new unity. “All that believed were together” — one in faith, in purpose, in love; and out of that oneness flowed the holding of all things common. The witnesses are careful to define it: not a system of enforced communism, nor the abolition of private property, but a spontaneous generosity that held possessions loosely and ready for the common good. They mark Ellicott’s point that here was the realising of Christ’s own teaching about treasure and almsgiving, a society built on sympathy and self-denial rather than self-interest. The verse thus shows the social fruit of Pentecost: the Spirit who filled them with God also knit them to one another, so that the love of God overflowed in the love of the brethren. The next verse will describe how this love expressed itself in practice — in the selling of goods to supply every need.

So the witnesses see in the believers’ sharing the fruit of their new unity. “All that believed were together” — one in faith, purpose, and love; and out of that oneness flowed the holding of all things common. The witnesses are careful to define it: not enforced communism nor the abolition of property, but a spontaneous generosity that held possessions loosely for the common good. Here was the realising of Christ’s teaching about treasure, a society built on sympathy rather than self-interest — the Spirit who filled them with God also knitting them to one another.
45:
On Acts 2:45 — “And sold their possessions and goods, and parted them to all men, as every man had need” — the witnesses mark the practical charity.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imperfect tenses. Robertson: “sold” and “parted” are imperfect — “a habit or custom from time to time,” distributing “according as any man had need.” Barnes distinguishes “possessions” (lands, houses) from movable “goods.” Wesley: many did this “long after… not that there was any positive command… love constrained them.”

The verse shows the love of the believers in action. They sold lands and goods and shared the proceeds, giving to each as he had need. The fellowship of hearts became a fellowship of substance, meeting want with willing generosity.

So the witnesses see the unity of the believers bear practical fruit in open-handed charity. The witnesses note the force of the imperfect tenses: this was not a single dramatic act of liquidation but an ongoing practice — selling and sharing from time to time as needs arose, so that none among them lacked. They mark Wesley’s point that no law compelled it; love constrained them, and the practice continued well beyond the first days. The distribution was measured by need, not divided into equal shares, showing a wise as well as a generous love. The verse thus displays the social ethic of the Spirit-filled church: a community in which the love of God so possessed the believers that they counted their goods as held in trust for the needy brother. It is the pattern, not necessarily of common ownership, but of a love that holds wealth loosely and gives freely as the needs of the saints require.

46:
On Acts 2:46 — “And they, continuing daily with one accord in the temple, and breaking bread from house to house, did eat their meat with gladness and singleness of heart” — the witnesses mark the joyful daily life.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the temple and the homes. Robertson: “still worshipping in the temple for no breach had yet come” with the Jews; daily “breaking bread at home… the regular meal.” Ellicott marks it “natural” that they did not keep aloof from the sanctuary. The Reformed Calvin: they frequented the temple “because there was more opportunity… to further the gospel,” not from the holiness of the place. Wesley: they carried “the same happy and holy temper through all their common actions.”

The verse pictures the rhythm of the believers’ days. With one accord they met daily in the temple and broke bread in their homes, eating together with gladness and simplicity of heart. Public worship and household fellowship, joy and singleness, fill their common life.

So the witnesses see the daily life of the first church as a blend of devotion and gladness. They continued in the temple — for as yet no rupture had come with Judaism, and the temple gave both a place of prayer and an opportunity for the gospel — and they broke bread from house to house, sharing meals and fellowship. The witnesses note the lovely temper of it: “gladness and singleness of heart,” a joyful simplicity that pervaded even their ordinary meals. They mark Wesley’s point that the same holy spirit ran through all their common actions, eating and working as they prayed. The verse thus shows the fruit of Pentecost in the texture of everyday life: a community at once devout and joyful, worshipping publicly and loving privately, their hearts so filled and united that gladness marked their daily bread. It is the picture of a church living in the freshness of its first love.

47:
On Acts 2:47 — “Praising God, and having favour with all the people. And the Lord added to the church daily such as should be saved” — the witnesses mark the close of the picture.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the favour and the growth. Barnes: “their humble, serious, and devoted lives won the favour of the great mass” (Luke 2:52). Robertson: “added” (imperfect, “kept on adding”); “if the Lord only always ‘added’ those who join our churches.” Ellicott: the “new life… had revived the early popularity of their Master with the common people.” The Reformed Calvin: “this is the fruit of an innocent life, to find favour even amongst strangers.”

The verse closes the portrait of the primitive church with praise, favour, and growth. The believers praised God and won the goodwill of the people; and the Lord himself kept adding daily to their number those who were being saved. The church grows by divine increase.

So the witnesses see the young church flourishing under God’s hand. Its life was praise — a community continually magnifying God; its reputation was favour — the winsome holiness of the believers commending them to the common people, even as the Sadducean rulers eyed them with fear. And its growth was the Lord’s own work: he “added” daily, and added “such as should be saved.” The witnesses note Robertson’s wistful comment that the Lord’s adding is the true increase of the church, and Calvin’s that an innocent life wins favour even among strangers. They mark that the converts were those being saved — the increase was not mere numbers but genuine conversions, the fruit of grace. The verse thus rounds out the Pentecost narrative: from the outpoured Spirit to a praising, beloved, and growing church, increased daily by the Lord himself — the living proof that the exalted Christ builds his Church by his Spirit.
